summary:
The Shimao site in Shaanxi province, northern China, occupied approximately 2300–2000 BC during the late Longshan period, is one of the largest and most impressive prehistoric stone-walled settlements in the world, covering nearly 4 square kilometres. Discovered and excavated from around 2012 onward, Shimao has transformed scholarly understanding of prehistoric Chinese civilisation, revealing a powerful regional polity in the Ordos region of northern China that was previously little known.

The outer stone wall of Shimao stretches for approximately 5.7 kilometres and encloses a stepped pyramid-like mound at the site's centre that rises to a height of about 70 metres in 11 tiers, covering approximately 8 hectares. This structure, one of the largest of its kind in the ancient world, served as an elite residential and ceremonial centre. Excavations on and around the pyramid have uncovered jade artefacts, painted murals, carved stone faces built into the wall, luxury ceramics, and evidence of human sacrifice — the skulls of sacrificed individuals were deposited in the stone foundations of the walls.

Shimao appears to have been the centre of a substantial regional polity with extensive trade connections: jade from distant sources, silk fragments, and turquoise objects indicate wide commercial and cultural networks. Its influence can be traced in archaeological sites across a broad area of northern China. The site's discovery has raised important questions about the relationship between contemporaneous regional cultures in China and the trajectory of early Chinese civilisation, suggesting that the Longshan-period China was characterised by multiple regional centres of power rather than a single dominant core.
